Um, just on, you've made a comment there,
um, on key section about protected Maori land.
I just wonder if you could spell out this, um, in your 11 D.
So the definition of protected Maori land in section 11
of the Infrastructure Financing Act includes previously held
Maori free old land that has cease to have
that status in accordance with any order
of the Maori land court.
If an order has been made over such land, then
that land should not have protected status.
Can you just walk us through
what that actually is all about?
That was my colleague's point.
Um, so that was Nick Kearney, I think, who,
who, who made that point.
And I, I wonder whether he's, he's, um, coming from the,
I'm not sure he is coming from the perspective of whether
Maori land should be, should,
should be treated any different differently
where it's critical infrastructure. Um,
Well, well I, I, I took what was being said there
as being at one stage it was protected,
and then there's a order of the Maori land court,
which makes it not protected anymore.
I, I mean, that's not a process I'm familiar with
or what circumstances that might apply.
I think you can, you can apply
to have Maori land converted into general land.
Uh, I guess the reasons why people do that sometimes it, it,
it's, it's easier to develop the land, easier to get, uh,
ka borrow money against it if it's general land
because of the limited ability to, um,
alienate Maori land.
It makes it less appealing for lenders sometimes.
So I'm, I'm not sure,
but if, if it became general land, I guess it would then
be subject to the same, um, you know, the same fast track
process and the same, um, you know,
submission and, uh, process.
Um, whereas I think you are trying to exempt Maori land from
that fast track processes that, is that right?
Well, I think it's the issue of the def what of
what actually is the definition of Maori land in this,
in this instance, if it's, it was
and now it isn't anymore in that.
Well, I think as soon as it's general land,
it's no longer Maori land, so,
Okay. Uh,
I don't, I'd have to check with Nick.
I, I don't, I don't really actually see that as
Okay. I
personally didn't see that as important. Okay.
Uh, that important. And the main thing for us,
I think was the removal of the ability
to at least go before a court.
And I guess the only way you could get
to a court now would be to require a judicial review of the
way that the submissions
and the objection process proceeded and the outcome of that
Look. The other
question I wanted to ask is,
you said you've acted both central government
and local government and for landowners, um,
I'm just interested in your observations around the, uh,
the emotional process for, um, for landowners and,
and so, you know, whether it's good, bad or indifferent.
You said it is, it's improved over the years.
Can you just fill us in a little bit on, on that please?
Uh, I think every landowner I deal with, um,
uh, they're um, they're, they're usually devastated
by the fact that they're, they, they're very, they, they're,
they're often as we all are very, very attached
to their piece of land and their home and,
and all the stuff that they've done over the years there
and the family and the, and,
and the effort to keep the, you know,
the property going and all that.
And so they're quite, they're,
they're very heavily invested, obviously, as you would be,
and that their major asset.
And so when it does, when it, when, when it's,
it's always a shock when they, you know,
even though they know there might be a project, um,
it's always a shock when they're approached
by the accredited supplier for their, you know,
for the first couple of meetings to talk them
through the process of the land actually being taken.
And often, often it's hard for them to see another, um,
an opportunity to get a substitute property, which,
you know, will replace what they've already got.
And, um, they don't often understand the process.
So it's really important, I think
that they get proper legal representation from someone who's
actually opened the Public Works Act
and familiar with all the ins and and outs of it.
And, uh, I think that does alleviate the stress
to some extent once they've got, uh,
once they've got a lawyer that knows the ins
and outs of the process and can take away the front facing,
dealing with the accredit supplier
and land information New Zealand and Transport Agency
or Auckland Transport or water care or, or whoever.
Um, and I,
and I think that, um,
it's a hugely emotional situation.
Um, and, and,
and I only sort of, it's only when I tell 'em about
what their rights are, and they may have already heard
this from the accredit supplier.
It's only when I tell 'em, I'm on your side.
I've been through this mul multiple times, I know how
to get you the maximum compensation that you're entitled
to under the public works.
This is how it's gonna, this is how it's gonna progress
that we've got valuers who are experienced at valuing land.
And the other ju the other issue obviously is always, I,
I mean obviously I think my properties are worth more than,
than what they probably are.
And, and often where the, where,
where traditionally big issues have been is
that the valuation
that the crown gets is often significantly lower than the
valuation that the landowner gets.
And that's where a lot of tension comes in
because you're trying to argue between the two valuations.
And so I guess I'd be a proponent
for saying well get one valuation from a, a reputable, uh,
and very competent and experienced valuer in that space
and run with that
because there's case law that says
that the government agencies shouldn't be nakedly.
I think the word nakedly must have
been an old case, I think.
And if there's any doubt about the value of the property,
then you should, um, you should, um,
settle generously in favor of the hire
of the two valuations.
And I think that could be written into the Public Works Act.
I think that would be good. And, um, what, what I perceive

So, um, they, they deserve to be looked
after in a very, um, almost a Rolls Royce sort
of red carpet fashion.
Um, and, and so over the years I've seen valuations,
very low valuations, the government have got high

that leads to delays of months
and months while you work out, well, what's it really worth?
And you, you try and reach an agreement on that

And, and basically my clients used to get, maybe not

Um, and instead of having a client that, or a landowner

and shortchanged at the other end.
And, um, so you're more likely to have litigants
and you're more likely to have delays.
You're more likely to have a project
that doesn't get started on time
and it gets built, you know, you know, delays and delays

So it's kind of like a false economy. Yeah,
I guess so. Thank you.
I think actually that's, I, I let to,
to let you go on that one, but it's, um,
but I think that's very valuable

and also some of those issues around the valuation process.
So thank you. We really appreciate that.
Thanks for your time. Thank very much. Thank you. Okay.

source_metadata.description:
Thursday 19 June 2025 | Transport and Infrastructure Committee part 5

Public Works (Critical Infrastructure) Amendment Bill

Hearing of evidence:

Amokura Panoho 
TeTakinga New
Pirirakau Tribal Authority - Incorporated (Jason Ake)               
Alvina Edwards
John McDonald-Wharry
The New Zealand Initiative (Dr Bryce Wilkinson)
Infrastructure New Zealand (Michelle McCormick, Policy Director)
Te Runanga o Toa Rangatira (Patrica Grace)
The Law Association of New Zealand (Phil Shannan)


Additional hearings for this bill can be found on 2025/06/19 Part 3 and the hearing from 2025/06/23
